title: Second Pastor at Frisco Megachurch Resigns Over 'Sexual Sin and Moral Failure' in 2025
teaser: John McKinzie, lead pastor of Hope Fellowship Church in Frisco, Texas, resigned on September 14, 2025 after confessing to sexual sin and moral failure before church leadership. He was the second pastor at the same megachurch to step down over similar circumstances within the same calendar year.
body:
There is a particular kind of institutional discomfort that comes with needing to send the same memo twice. KERA News reported on September 16, 2025 that John McKinzie, 59, the lead pastor of Hope Fellowship Church in Frisco, Texas, had stepped down from his position two days earlier, on September 14, 2025. His resignation came after he confessed to what church leadership described as sexual sin and moral failure. The congregation was, by any reasonable count, already familiar with this particular genre of announcement.

McKinzie disclosed his conduct directly to church leadership before the news reached anyone else on staff. According to church elders, no one in the organization had any prior knowledge of the moral failure before McKinzie came forward himself. This voluntary disclosure is the kind of detail a congregation's communications team tends to emphasize, and to their credit, it does represent a distinction from scenarios involving external exposure. It does not, however, substantially alter the underlying facts of the situation.

What makes this resignation structurally remarkable is not the confession itself, but the sequencing. McKinzie was the second pastor at Hope Fellowship to resign over similar circumstances in 2025. The year was not yet over when the second departure occurred in September. The same church, the same category of failure, within a single calendar year: that is a pattern that warrants attention beyond the pastoral level.

Hope Fellowship operates as a megachurch, a designation that typically implies a congregation in the thousands, a professional ministry staff, formal accountability structures, and the institutional resources to model the values preached from the pulpit. Megachurches often cultivate reputations as centers of moral formation and family values, particularly in the Dallas-Fort Worth evangelical corridor, where Hope Fellowship is situated. The gap between that public identity and the private conduct of its leadership is the central tension of this story.

Church elders issued a statement addressing the resignation, confirming the nature of the disclosure and asserting that no staff member had prior knowledge. The framing was careful, as such statements typically are, calibrated to acknowledge wrongdoing while containing reputational damage. What the statement could not contain, at least not fully, was the compounding effect of a second leadership failure of the same type in the same institution within the same year.

McKinzie had served as lead pastor, the senior-most spiritual authority at Hope Fellowship. That role carries an explicit expectation of moral accountability, a standard the church itself presumably articulates in its doctrinal commitments and membership covenants. The irony of an institution built around the formation of virtue producing two sequential pastoral resignations for sexual misconduct is not subtle. It is, however, worth stating plainly rather than euphemistically.

The phrase used by church leadership, sexual sin and moral failure, is a formulation common in evangelical resignation announcements. It is deliberately broad, conveying seriousness without specifying the nature or scope of the conduct. This language serves a pastoral function for congregations navigating public disclosure, but it also limits accountability. Without specificity, the congregation, and the public, cannot fully assess the severity of the situation or the adequacy of any institutional response.

One question that naturally arises following a second resignation of this type is whether Hope Fellowship's internal accountability structures are functioning as designed. The elders' statement that no staff member had prior knowledge before McKinzie disclosed his conduct suggests the failure was concealed, at least internally, until the pastor chose to come forward. Whether that concealment was possible because of structural gaps, cultural norms within the organization, or simply the private nature of the conduct is not answered by the available reporting.

It is worth noting that McKinzie's age, 59, places him well within the phase of life and ministry where institutional authority is most consolidated and external checks are often most relaxed. Senior pastors of large churches tend to occupy positions of significant social power within their communities, with accountability structures that are nominally present but sometimes difficult to activate in practice. That dynamic is not unique to Hope Fellowship, but it is relevant context for understanding how these situations develop.

The congregation of Hope Fellowship now faces the task of processing a second leadership collapse within a single year. For many members, particularly those who joined or remained because of McKinzie's specific ministry, the pastoral relationship was a significant part of their spiritual community. The collateral damage of these disclosures extends well beyond the individuals directly involved, into the trust structures that congregational life depends upon.

Accountability journalism does not require a villain, only a contradiction. The contradiction here is straightforward: a lead pastor of a Christian megachurch that presumably teaches sexual fidelity and moral integrity failed, by his own admission, to live according to those standards. The church's elders are now managing the second such failure in 2025. The congregation is owed transparency proportionate to the trust they extended, and the institution is overdue for a structural accounting of how this pattern became possible.

McKinzie's resignation stands at a Suspended License level on the ClownBinge Self-Own Scale, a score reflecting not just personal hypocrisy but the institutional dimension of a second failure of the same type within the same year. The first resignation may have been an isolated incident. The second one is a data point in a pattern, and patterns require more than pastoral care. They require honest institutional reckoning.
